input.prompt:
You are performing an ADVERSARIAL CODE REVIEW (Round 3) for Story 1-3: Deploy NAT Gateway and Public IP Resources. This is an Azure Terraform infrastructure project for a Golden Image AVD pipeline.

TWO PRIOR REVIEWS already fixed many issues. Your job is to find what they MISSED. Be ruthless — find 3-10 specific, actionable issues minimum.

## Story Acceptance Criteria
1. NAT Gateway provisioned using AVM `Azure/avm-res-network-natgateway/azurerm` v0.2.1 with Standard SKU, 4-min idle timeout
2. Static Standard public IP and /30 IP prefix assigned to NAT Gateway
3. NAT Gateway associated with PrivateSubnet and PublicSubnet
4. Public IPs via `for_each` over `local.public_ip_definitions` with Standard SKU, Static, Regional, IPv4
5. NAT GW public IP address and prefix CIDR exposed as outputs
6. All resources receive `local.common_tags`

## Prior Reviews Fixed (do NOT re-report these):
- Round 1: Dead variables wired, location inconsistency, PIP idle timeout, unused module removed, outputs consolidated, Environment tag
- Round 2: inherit_tags no-op, zone mismatch on PIP, IP prefix zones, lifecycle prevent_destroy, PIP idle timeout alignment, hyphenated resource label renamed, PrivateSubnet service endpoints, parameterized NAT GW idle timeout, public_ip_prefix_length=0

## Files to Review

### terraform/natg.tf
```hcl
module "avm_nat_gateway" {
  source  = "Azure/avm-res-network-natgateway/azurerm"
  version = "0.2.1"

  name                    = local.nat_gateway_name
  location                = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.location
  resource_group_name     = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.name
  idle_timeout_in_minutes = var.nat_gateway_idle_timeout_in_minutes
  sku_name                = "Standard"
  tags                    = local.common_tags
  enable_telemetry        = false
  zones = null

  public_ip_prefix_length = 0

  public_ips = {
    "packer_image_natg_pip" = {
      name = local.nat_gateway_pip_name
    }
  }

  public_ip_configuration = {
    allocation_method       = var.nat_gateway_pip_allocation_method
    sku                     = var.nat_gateway_pip_sku
    zones                   = var.nat_gateway_pip_zones
    ddos_protection_mode    = var.nat_gateway_pip_ddos_protection_mode
    idle_timeout_in_minutes = var.nat_gateway_pip_idle_timeout_in_minutes
  }
}

resource "azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_association" "natg_prefix" {
  nat_gateway_id      = module.avm_nat_gateway.resource_id
  public_ip_prefix_id = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix.id
}
```

### terraform/main.tf
```hcl
resource "azurerm_public_ip_prefix" "packer_image_natg_ipprefix" {
  tags                = local.common_tags
  sku_tier            = "Regional"
  sku                 = "Standard"
  resource_group_name = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.name
  prefix_length       = var.nat_gateway_pip_prefix_length
  name                = local.nat_gateway_pip_prefix_name
  location            = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.location
  ip_version          = "IPv4"
  zones = null

  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true
  }
}

moved {
  from = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer-image-natg-ipprefix
  to   = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix
}
```

### terraform/networking.tf
```hcl
module "virtualnetwork" {
  source  = "Azure/avm-res-network-virtualnetwork/azurerm"
  version = "~> 0.2"

  name                = local.vnet_name
  location            = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.location
  resource_group_name = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.name
  address_space       = var.vnet_address_space
  tags                = local.common_tags

  subnets = {
    GatewaySubnet = {
      name             = "GatewaySubnet"
      address_prefixes = var.vnet_subnet_gateway
    }

    AzureBastionSubnet = {
      name             = "AzureBastionSubnet"
      address_prefixes = var.vnet_azure_subnet_bastion
      network_security_group = {
        id = module.bastion_nsg_avm.resource_id
      }
    }

    PublicSubnet = {
      name             = local.subnet_public_name
      address_prefixes = var.vnet_subnet_public
      network_security_group = {
        id = module.public_nsg_avm.resource_id
      }
      nat_gateway = {
        id = module.avm_nat_gateway.resource_id
      }
      service_endpoints = [
        "Microsoft.ContainerRegistry",
        "Microsoft.KeyVault",
        "Microsoft.ServiceBus",
        "Microsoft.Storage"
      ]
    }

    PrivateSubnet = {
      name             = local.subnet_private_name
      address_prefixes = var.vnet_subnet_private
      network_security_group = {
        id = module.private_nsg_avm.resource_id
      }
      nat_gateway = {
        id = module.avm_nat_gateway.resource_id
      }
      service_endpoints = [
        "Microsoft.ContainerRegistry",
        "Microsoft.KeyVault",
        "Microsoft.Storage"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### terraform/public_ips.tf
```hcl
resource "azurerm_public_ip" "pips" {
  for_each = local.public_ip_definitions

  name                    = each.value.name
  resource_group_name     = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.name
  location                = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.location
  sku_tier                = "Regional"
  sku                     = "Standard"
  allocation_method       = "Static"
  ip_version              = "IPv4"
  idle_timeout_in_minutes = 4
  ddos_protection_mode    = "VirtualNetworkInherited"
  domain_name_label       = each.value.domain_name_label
  tags                    = each.value.apply_common_tags ? local.common_tags : null
}
```

### terraform/outputs.tf (NAT Gateway section)
```hcl
output "nat_gateway_id" {
  description = "The ID of the AVM NAT Gateway."
  value       = module.avm_nat_gateway.resource_id
}

output "nat_gateway_public_ip_address" {
  description = "The IP address of the Public IP created by the AVM NAT Gateway module."
  value       = module.avm_nat_gateway.public_ip_resource["packer_image_natg_pip"].ip_address
}

output "nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_value" {
  description = "The CIDR of the Public IP Prefix associated with the NAT Gateway."
  value       = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix.ip_prefix
}

output "nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_id" {
  description = "The resource ID of the Public IP Prefix associated with the NAT Gateway."
  value       = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix.id
}
```

### terraform/locals.tf
```hcl
locals {
  common_tags = {
    CDC                            = "INFRA_SO"
    Account                        = "infraestrutura"
    Application                    = "DevOps"
    ApplicationName                = "Packer Image Builder"
    Applicationversion             = "1.0.0"
    AccessControlContext           = "INFRA_SO"
    AuditRequirement               = "No"
    BusinessUnit                   = "INFRA_SO"
    Compliance                     = "HIPAA"
    Confidentiality                = "PII/PHI"
    CostCenter                     = "15260"
    CreateDate                     = "2023-10-01"
    DataSensitivity                = "PII/PHI"
    DataSubject                    = "INFRA_SO"
    Environment                    = var.environment
    ImageTemplateName              = "it-avd-aib-template-01"
    ImageTemplateResourceGroupName = "rg-hypera-packer-image"
    Infrastructureversion          = "1.0.0"
    Name                           = "DevOps Teams"
    OperatingCostCenter            = "0570025003"
    Owner                          = "INFRA_SO"
    ProjectCostCenter              = "0570025003"
    Projeto                        = "INFRA_SO"
    ProjectCode                    = "HYP-007"
    RetentionPolicy                = "7-years-PHI"
    SecurityContact                = "juliano.barbosa@hypera.com.br"
    Source                         = "terraform-avm-module"
    Team                           = "DevOps"
    Tier                           = "Infrastructure"
    UsedBy                         = "AzureVMImageBuilder"
  }

  public_ip_definitions = {
    "bastion" = {
      name              = local.pip_bastion_name
      domain_name_label = null
      apply_common_tags = true
    }
  }
}
```

### terraform/variables.tf (NAT Gateway section)
```hcl
variable "nat_gateway_pip_prefix_length" {
  type        = number
  description = "Prefix length for the NAT Gateway Public IP. Azure accepts 28-31."
  default     = 30
  validation {
    condition     = contains([28, 29, 30, 31], var.nat_gateway_pip_prefix_length)
    error_message = "Prefix length must be 28, 29, 30, or 31 for Azure public IP prefixes."
  }
}

variable "nat_gateway_pip_sku" {
  type        = string
  description = "SKU for the NAT Gateway Public IP. NAT Gateway requires Standard."
  default     = "Standard"
  validation {
    condition     = var.nat_gateway_pip_sku == "Standard"
    error_message = "NAT Gateway public IP must use Standard SKU."
  }
}

variable "nat_gateway_pip_allocation_method" {
  type        = string
  description = "Allocation method for the NAT Gateway Public IP. Standard SKU requires Static."
  default     = "Static"
  validation {
    condition     = var.nat_gateway_pip_allocation_method == "Static"
    error_message = "Standard SKU public IPs require Static allocation method."
  }
}

variable "nat_gateway_idle_timeout_in_minutes" {
  type        = number
  description = "Idle timeout in minutes for the NAT Gateway itself."
  default     = 4
  validation {
    condition     = var.nat_gateway_idle_timeout_in_minutes >= 4 && var.nat_gateway_idle_timeout_in_minutes <= 120
    error_message = "NAT Gateway idle timeout must be between 4 and 120 minutes."
  }
}

variable "nat_gateway_pip_zones" {
  type        = list(string)
  description = "Availability zones for the NAT Gateway Public IP."
  default     = []
  nullable    = false
}

variable "nat_gateway_pip_ddos_protection_mode" {
  type        = string
  description = "DDoS protection mode for the NAT Gateway Public IP."
  default     = "VirtualNetworkInherited"
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["Disabled", "Enabled", "VirtualNetworkInherited"], var.nat_gateway_pip_ddos_protection_mode)
    error_message = "DDoS protection mode must be Disabled, Enabled, or VirtualNetworkInherited."
  }
}

variable "nat_gateway_pip_idle_timeout_in_minutes" {
  type        = number
  description = "Idle timeout in minutes for the NAT Gateway Public IP."
  default     = 4
  validation {
    condition     = var.nat_gateway_pip_idle_timeout_in_minutes >= 4 && var.nat_gateway_pip_idle_timeout_in_minutes <= 30
    error_message = "Idle timeout must be between 4 and 30 minutes."
  }
}
```

### terraform/terraform.tfvars (NAT section)
```hcl
nat_gateway_idle_timeout_in_minutes     = 4
nat_gateway_pip_prefix_length           = 30
nat_gateway_pip_sku                     = "Standard"
nat_gateway_pip_allocation_method       = "Static"
nat_gateway_pip_zones                   = []
nat_gateway_pip_ddos_protection_mode    = "VirtualNetworkInherited"
nat_gateway_pip_idle_timeout_in_minutes = 4
```

### terraform/name.tf (NAT section)
```hcl
locals {
  nat_gateway_name            = "natg-${var.resource_group_name}-${var.environment}-${var.azure_region_shortcode}"
  nat_gateway_pip_name        = "natg-pip-${var.resource_group_name}-${var.environment}-${var.azure_region_shortcode}"
  nat_gateway_pip_prefix_name = "natg-pip-prefix-${var.resource_group_name}-${var.environment}-${var.azure_region_shortcode}"
}
```

## Your Task
Perform a RUTHLESS adversarial review. Check:
1. **AC Validation** — Is every acceptance criterion ACTUALLY met?
2. **Security** — Any HIPAA/compliance gaps? Sensitive data exposure?
3. **Terraform Best Practices** — Resource naming, module usage, state safety
4. **Azure Best Practices** — NAT Gateway limits, subnet sizing, zone alignment
5. **Code Quality** — DRY violations, hardcoded values, missing validation
6. **Edge Cases** — What breaks on `terraform destroy`? On module upgrade? On multi-region?
7. **Integration Issues** — Cross-resource dependencies, circular references, ordering

Format each finding as:
```
[SEVERITY: CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW] Title
File: path:line
Issue: What's wrong
Evidence: Why it's wrong (reference docs/specs)
Fix: Specific remediation
```

DO NOT REPORT issues already fixed in Round 1 or Round 2. Find NEW issues only.

    error_message = "Prefix length must be 28, 29, 30, or 31 for Azure public IP prefixes."
  }
}

variable "nat_gateway_pip_sku" {
  type        = string
  description = "SKU for the NAT Gateway Public IP. NAT Gateway requires Standard."
  default     = "Standard"
  validation {
    condition     = var.nat_gateway_pip_sku == "Standard"
    error_message = "NAT Gateway public IP must use Standard SKU."
  }
}

variable "nat_gateway_pip_allocation_method" {
  type        = string
  description = "Allocation method for the NAT Gateway Public IP. Standard SKU requires Static."
  default     = "Static"
  validation {
    condition     = var.nat_gateway_pip_allocation_method == "Static"
    error_message = "Standard SKU public IPs require Static allocation method."
  }

variable "nat_gateway_idle_timeout_in_minutes" {
  type        = number
  description = "Idle timeout in minutes for the NAT Gateway itself."
  default     = 4
  validation {
    condition     = var.nat_gateway_idle_timeout_in_minutes >= 4 && var.nat_gateway_idle_timeout_in_minutes <= 120
    error_message = "NAT Gateway idle timeout must be between 4 and 120 minutes."
  }

variable "nat_gateway_pip_zones" {

TASK: Round 3 adversarial code review for story 1-3

---

## Adversarial Code Review - Round 3 Findings

After thorough analysis of all files and cross-referencing with acceptance criteria, Azure documentation, and Terraform best practices, here are the NEW issues not caught by Round 1 or Round 2:

---

### [SEVERITY: CRITICAL] Secrets Committed to Version Control in terraform.tfvars
File: terraform/terraform.tfvars:36-40
Issue: The file contains a plaintext Microsoft Teams webhook URL (line 36) and an Azure subscription ID embedded in `custom_image_id` (line 40). This file is tracked by git (confirmed via `git ls-files`) and `.gitignore` does not exclude `*.tfvars`. The webhook URL is a bearer-style credential — anyone with it can post messages to the Teams channel.
Evidence: `msteams_channel = "https://hypermarcas.webhook.office.com/webhookb2/..."` is a secret URL. HIPAA compliance (tagged on every resource) requires protection of infrastructure credentials. The `.gitignore` only excludes `.terraform`, `.venv`, and `node_modules`.
Fix: Add `*.tfvars` to `.gitignore` (or at minimum `terraform.tfvars`). Move secrets to environment variables, Azure Key Vault references, or a `.tfvars` file excluded from source control. Rotate the exposed webhook URL. Consider using `sensitive = true` on the `msteams_channel` variable.

---

### [SEVERITY: CRITICAL] NAT Gateway Public IP Output Lacks `sensitive` Flag Despite HIPAA Context
File: terraform/outputs.tf:53-55
Issue: `nat_gateway_public_ip_address` exposes the static public IP address in plaintext Terraform output. In a HIPAA environment with this IP likely allowlisted at third-party services, the IP address is operationally sensitive. None of the NAT gateway or public IP outputs are marked `sensitive = true`, meaning they appear in CLI output, state files, and CI/CD logs.
Evidence: The `common_tags` include `Compliance = "HIPAA"`, `DataSensitivity = "PII/PHI"`, and `RetentionPolicy = "7-years-PHI"`. Public IP addresses of NAT gateways used for allowlisting are infrastructure secrets in regulated environments. Terraform documentation recommends marking infrastructure endpoint outputs as sensitive when they could aid reconnaissance.
Fix: Add `sensitive = true` to `nat_gateway_public_ip_address` and `nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_value` outputs. Consider the same for `all_public_ip_addresses`.

---

### [SEVERITY: HIGH] NAT Gateway PIP Created by AVM Module Lacks `prevent_destroy` Lifecycle
File: terraform/natg.tf:22-27
Issue: The `public_ips` block creates a PIP inside the AVM module. Unlike the IP prefix (which has `prevent_destroy = true` in `main.tf`), this module-managed PIP has no lifecycle protection. If the module is accidentally removed or the key `"packer_image_natg_pip"` is renamed, the static public IP is destroyed and a new IP is allocated. Any firewall allowlists referencing the old IP break.
Evidence: The IP prefix has `prevent_destroy = true` (added in Round 2) specifically because "IP prefix allocates public IPs from Azure. Destroying releases IPs permanently." The same logic applies to the NAT Gateway's own PIP. The AVM module v0.2.1 does not expose lifecycle configuration for module-created PIPs.
Fix: Consider managing the NAT Gateway PIP externally (like the IP prefix) via `azurerm_public_ip` resource with `prevent_destroy = true`, then passing its ID into the module. Alternatively, document this risk and add a warning comment that the module-managed PIP cannot be lifecycle-protected.

---

### [SEVERITY: HIGH] `public_ips.tf` PIPs Missing `zones` Attribute - Inconsistent with Zone Strategy
File: terraform/public_ips.tf:1-15
Issue: The `azurerm_public_ip.pips` resource (currently creating the bastion PIP) does not specify a `zones` attribute. When omitted on `azurerm_public_ip` with Standard SKU, Azure defaults to zone-redundant (`["1","2","3"]`). This is inconsistent with the explicit non-zonal (`zones = null` / `zones = []`) strategy documented via ADRs on the NAT Gateway, its PIP, and IP prefix.
Evidence: Round 2 specifically fixed zone alignment across NAT GW (null), PIP (empty list), and IP prefix (null) with ADR comments stating "zones must align" and "non-zonal (regional) for dev." But the bastion PIP in `public_ips.tf` was never reviewed for zone consistency. Azure docs state Standard SKU PIPs default to zone-redundant when `zones` is omitted.
Fix: Add `zones = []` (or `zones = null`) to the `azurerm_public_ip.pips` resource to explicitly declare non-zonal, consistent with the project's zone strategy. Consider parameterizing this via the `public_ip_definitions` local.

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] Hardcoded Values in `public_ips.tf` Violate DRY - Not Parameterized Like NAT GW PIPs
File: terraform/public_ips.tf:7-12
Issue: The bastion PIP hardcodes `sku = "Standard"`, `allocation_method = "Static"`, `idle_timeout_in_minutes = 4`, `ddos_protection_mode = "VirtualNetworkInherited"` directly in the resource. Meanwhile, Round 1 and Round 2 painstakingly parameterized identical values for the NAT Gateway PIP into variables with validation blocks. This creates a DRY violation where the same PIP configuration standards are expressed two different ways.
Evidence: Six variables exist for NAT GW PIP config (`nat_gateway_pip_sku`, `nat_gateway_pip_allocation_method`, etc.) with validation blocks. The bastion PIP ignores all of them and hardcodes the same values. If the org standard changes (e.g., DDoS mode), only the NAT GW PIP would be updated via variables.
Fix: Either create shared PIP variables/locals that both resources reference, or add the PIP properties (`sku`, `allocation_method`, `idle_timeout`, `ddos_protection_mode`) to the `public_ip_definitions` map so each PIP entry can specify its own values (or fall back to defaults).

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] VNet Module Version Constraint `~> 0.2` Allows Breaking Minor Updates
File: terraform/networking.tf:8
Issue: The VNet AVM module uses `version = "~> 0.2"` (pessimistic constraint allowing any 0.x.y where x >= 2). For 0.x versions, SemVer treats minor bumps as potentially breaking. The NAT Gateway module is correctly pinned to exact `0.2.1`. This inconsistency means `terraform init -upgrade` could pull a new VNet module version that changes subnet schema, breaking NAT gateway associations.
Evidence: AVM modules at 0.x are pre-1.0 and follow different SemVer conventions where 0.3.0 can break 0.2.x APIs. The story's anti-patterns section says "Do NOT change the NAT Gateway module version (pinned to 0.2.1)" but the VNet module's loose constraint could self-update. The subnet `nat_gateway = { id = ... }` syntax could change between 0.2 and 0.3.
Fix: Pin the VNet module to an exact version (e.g., `version = "0.2.3"`) consistent with the NAT Gateway module's pinning strategy. At minimum, use `version = "~> 0.2.0"` to restrict to patch-level updates only.

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] Missing `moved` Block Documentation - Name Change Will Destroy/Recreate IP Prefix
File: terraform/main.tf:22-26, story notes line 100
Issue: The `moved` block migrates from `packer-image-natg-ipprefix` to `packer_image_natg_ipprefix` (label rename from Round 2). However, Task 4 also changed the *Azure resource name* from `"packer-image-natg-ipprefix"` to `local.nat_gateway_pip_prefix_name` which resolves to `"natg-pip-prefix-rg-hypera-packer-image-dev-eus2"`. The `moved` block handles state migration for the Terraform label, but the Azure-side name change forces a destroy/recreate of the IP prefix resource -- directly contradicting `prevent_destroy = true` which will cause a Terraform error on apply.
Evidence: The story's own Dev Notes warn at line 100: "Changing this name will cause Terraform to destroy and recreate the IP prefix resource." The `prevent_destroy = true` lifecycle rule (added in Round 2) will cause `terraform apply` to fail with "Instance cannot be destroyed" because Terraform tries to destroy the old-named resource and create a new one. These two changes are mutually contradictory.
Fix: If the IP prefix already exists in Azure with the old name `packer-image-natg-ipprefix`, revert to the hardcoded name and remove the `moved` block, OR use `terraform state mv` and an Azure-side rename if supported. If the resource does NOT yet exist in Azure, the `moved` block is unnecessary (there is no state to migrate) and should be removed to reduce confusion. Add a comment documenting which scenario applies.

---

### [SEVERITY: LOW] `enable_telemetry = false` Only on NAT GW Module, Not VNet Module
File: terraform/natg.tf:11 vs terraform/networking.tf:10
Issue: The NAT Gateway module explicitly sets `enable_telemetry = false` while the VNet module has it commented out (`# enable_telemetry = false`). This means the VNet module sends telemetry to Microsoft while the NAT GW module does not. In a HIPAA environment, telemetry data transmission should be consistently controlled.
Evidence: The VNet module line 10 shows `# enable_telemetry    = false` (commented out). AVM modules send anonymous telemetry by default. The Round 2 deferred issues noted "enable_telemetry inconsistency" but it was never fixed.
Fix: Uncomment `enable_telemetry = false` in the VNet module, and audit all other AVM modules in the project for consistency.

---

### [SEVERITY: LOW] NAT Gateway Naming Convention Uses Non-Standard Prefix
File: terraform/name.tf:5-7
Issue: The NAT Gateway uses prefix `natg-` while Azure Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) recommends `ng-` for NAT Gateways, `pip-` for public IPs, and `ippre-` for IP prefixes. The current names (`natg-`, `natg-pip-`, `natg-pip-prefix-`) are project-specific but not aligned with CAF, making them harder for new team members or Azure support to identify.
Evidence: Microsoft CAF naming conventions: NAT Gateway = `ng-`, Public IP = `pip-`, Public IP Prefix = `ippre-`. Current naming diverges significantly.
Fix: This is a low-priority consistency issue. Document the naming convention choice in an ADR. If CAF alignment is desired, plan a coordinated rename with state migration in a future story.

---

### Summary Table

| # | Severity | Issue | File |
|---|----------|-------|------|
| 1 | CRITICAL | Secrets (webhook URL, subscription ID) committed in tfvars | terraform.tfvars |
| 2 | CRITICAL | Public IP outputs lack `sensitive = true` in HIPAA context | outputs.tf |
| 3 | HIGH | Module-managed NAT GW PIP has no lifecycle protection | natg.tf |
| 4 | HIGH | Bastion PIP missing explicit zones attribute | public_ips.tf |
| 5 | MEDIUM | Hardcoded PIP values vs parameterized NAT GW PIP (DRY) | public_ips.tf |
| 6 | MEDIUM | VNet module ~> 0.2 allows breaking updates | networking.tf |
| 7 | MEDIUM | `prevent_destroy` + name change = apply failure | main.tf |
| 8 | LOW | Telemetry inconsistency across AVM modules | natg.tf / networking.tf |
| 9 | LOW | Non-CAF naming convention undocumented | name.tf |

VERIFY: Nine new issues found across security, state safety, and consistency
TARS: Nine new issues found including two critical secrets and lifecycle gaps
```</result>

azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_association to link it. This gives you
full lifecycle control over the PIP. Example:
  resource "azurerm_public_ip" "natg_pip" {
    name                = local.nat_gateway_pip_name
    location            = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.location
    resource_group_name = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.name
    allocation_method   = "Static"

## Adversarial Code Review -- Round 3 Findings

After examining the actual AVM module source code (v0.2.1), all Terraform configuration files, variable definitions, and cross-referencing against Azure NAT Gateway documentation and HIPAA compliance requirements, here are the NEW issues that Rounds 1 and 2 missed.

---

### [SEVERITY: HIGH] NAT Gateway missing resource lock -- IP prefix protected but NAT GW itself is not

**File:** `terraform/natg.tf:1-43`

**Issue:** The `azurerm_public_ip_prefix` resource has `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` (good), but the NAT Gateway itself has no protection. The AVM module exposes a `lock` variable (type `object({ kind = string, name = optional(string) })`) that is not being used. If someone runs `terraform destroy` or removes the module block, the NAT Gateway and its module-managed PIP are destroyed without guardrails. In a HIPAA environment where the NAT GW's static IP is allowlisted at firewalls for PII/PHI data flows, destroying the NAT GW is equally catastrophic as destroying the IP prefix -- all outbound connectivity for both subnets dies instantly.

**Evidence:** AVM module `variables.tf` line 35-52 shows `var.lock` accepting `CanNotDelete` or `ReadOnly`. The IP prefix has `prevent_destroy` but the NAT Gateway that depends on it does not. Azure best practice for production networking resources is to apply `CanNotDelete` locks.

**Fix:** Add the lock parameter to the AVM module call:
```hcl
lock = {
  kind = "CanNotDelete"
  name = "natg-protect-hipaa-outbound"
}
```

---

### [SEVERITY: HIGH] Sensitive PII in common_tags exposed via outputs without sensitive flag

**File:** `terraform/outputs.tf:48-66` and `terraform/locals.tf:1-33`

**Issue:** `local.common_tags` contains `SecurityContact = "juliano.barbosa@hypera.com.br"` (a personal email address -- PII under HIPAA/LGPD). Every output that exposes a full resource object (like the NAT Gateway module outputs) can leak these tags in Terraform state and `terraform output` commands. While the individual outputs defined here only extract scalar values (IDs, IPs), the Terraform state file itself stores the full module resource attributes including all tags. More critically, the `all_public_ip_addresses`, `all_public_ip_ids`, and `all_public_ip_fqdns` outputs at lines 79-99 expose resource details without the `sensitive = true` flag. Anyone with state file access sees the email address in tag metadata.

**Evidence:** The `SecurityContact` tag contains a named individual's corporate email. Under HIPAA, organizational contact information tied to specific individuals constitutes workforce PII. The Terraform remote state in Azure Storage is accessible to anyone with the storage account key (which is passed via `-backend-config`).

**Fix:** Either (a) change `SecurityContact` to a distribution list (`infra-so@hypera.com.br`) instead of a named individual, or (b) add `sensitive = true` to outputs that could leak state, or (c) ensure the state backend storage account has proper RBAC and encryption-at-rest (verify separately).

---

### [SEVERITY: HIGH] VNet module version constraint is too loose -- allows breaking upgrades

**File:** `terraform/networking.tf:8`

**Issue:** The VNet module uses `version = "~> 0.2"` which allows any `0.x` minor version bump (0.3, 0.4, etc. up to 1.0). For AVM modules in the 0.x range, minor versions CAN include breaking changes per SemVer pre-1.0 rules ("Major version zero (0.y.z) is for initial development. Anything MAY change at any time"). Meanwhile, the NAT Gateway module is correctly pinned to exact version `0.2.1`. This inconsistency means `terraform init -upgrade` could pull a breaking VNet module version that changes subnet association behavior, potentially detaching the NAT Gateway from subnets and breaking HIPAA-compliant outbound routing.

**Evidence:** SemVer spec section 4: "Major version zero (0.y.z) is for initial development. Anything MAY change at any time. The public API SHOULD NOT be considered stable." The NAT GW module is pinned (`= "0.2.1"`) but the VNet module is not (`~> 0.2`), creating an asymmetric risk.

**Fix:** Pin the VNet module to an exact version:
```hcl
version = "0.2.0"  # or whatever the current resolved version is
```

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] NSG modules also use loose version pinning

**File:** `terraform/nsg.tf:3,30,55`

**Issue:** All three NSG AVM modules use `version = "0.4.0"` which IS exact-pinned (good). However, this is worth confirming the pinning is intentional vs. coincidental. No issue here on re-inspection -- withdrawing this finding.

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] No explicit deny-all outbound NSG rule on PrivateSubnet -- NAT GW provides unrestricted egress

**File:** `terraform/nsg.tf:1-25` and `terraform/terraform.tfvars:43-66`

**Issue:** The PrivateSubnet's NSG (`private_security_rules` in tfvars) only has two inbound rules (`Allow-Internal` at priority 100, `Allow-HTTPS` at priority 110). There are zero outbound rules defined. Azure NSGs have a default outbound rule (priority 65000) that allows ALL outbound traffic. Combined with the NAT Gateway association, this means ANY workload in the PrivateSubnet can reach ANY internet destination. For a HIPAA environment handling PII/PHI, the principle of least privilege demands explicit outbound restrictions. A compromised Packer build VM could exfiltrate data to any arbitrary endpoint.

**Evidence:** Azure documentation: "Default outbound security rules -- AllowInternetOutBound: Allow all outbound Internet traffic." HIPAA Security Rule 164.312(e)(1) requires technical security measures to guard against unauthorized access to ePHI transmitted over electronic communications networks. Unrestricted outbound through NAT GW violates this.

**Fix:** Add explicit outbound deny-all rule at a lower priority and only allow required destinations:
```hcl
{
  name                       = "Deny-All-Outbound"
  priority                   = 4096
  direction                  = "Outbound"
  access                     = "Deny"
  protocol                   = "*"
  source_port_range          = "*"
  destination_port_range     = "*"
  source_address_prefix      = "*"
  destination_address_prefix = "Internet"
  description                = "HIPAA: deny unrestricted outbound; allow only explicitly permitted destinations"
}
```
Then add specific allow rules for required destinations (Azure services, package repos, etc.) at higher priority.

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] Public IP prefix association creates implicit dependency but no explicit depends_on

**File:** `terraform/natg.tf:48-51`

**Issue:** The `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_association` references both `module.avm_nat_gateway.resource_id` and `azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix.id`. While Terraform can infer these data dependencies, the AVM module internally creates its own `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_association` for the PIP (line 36-41 of module's main.tf). If both the module's internal PIP association AND the external prefix association are created simultaneously during initial apply, Azure sometimes returns a conflict error (409) because two association operations hit the NAT Gateway concurrently. This is a race condition that manifests intermittently.

**Evidence:** Azure ARM has a concurrency limit on NAT Gateway association operations. The module's `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_association.this` and the root's `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_association.natg_prefix` both modify the same NAT Gateway resource. Terraform's parallelism can submit them simultaneously.

**Fix:** Add an explicit dependency to serialize the association operations:
```hcl
resource "azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_association" "natg_prefix" {
  nat_gateway_id      = module.avm_nat_gateway.resource_id
  public_ip_prefix_id = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix.id

  depends_on = [module.avm_nat_gateway]
}
```
The `depends_on` on the full module ensures all internal module resources (including the PIP association) complete before the prefix association begins.

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] Acceptance Criterion 4 not met -- NAT GW PIP not in `local.public_ip_definitions` for_each

**File:** `terraform/locals.tf:35-41` and `terraform/public_ips.tf:1-15`

**Issue:** Story acceptance criterion 4 states: "Public IPs via `for_each` over `local.public_ip_definitions` with Standard SKU, Static, Regional, IPv4." Currently, `local.public_ip_definitions` only contains the bastion PIP. The NAT Gateway PIP is created inside the AVM module via `public_ips` map, NOT via the `for_each` over `local.public_ip_definitions`. This means the NAT GW PIP does not follow the same pattern as other PIPs in the project, and technically does not satisfy AC4 as written. The NAT GW PIP's configuration (SKU, allocation, tier) is governed by `public_ip_configuration` in the module rather than the standardized `azurerm_public_ip.pips` resource.

**Evidence:** AC4 explicitly says "Public IPs via `for_each` over `local.public_ip_definitions`". The NAT GW PIP is created by the AVM module, not by this for_each. Whether this is acceptable depends on whether AC4 was intended to cover ALL PIPs or only non-module PIPs. This ambiguity should be resolved.

**Fix:** Either (a) clarify in the story that AC4 applies only to standalone PIPs (not module-managed ones), or (b) remove `public_ips` from the AVM module call, add the NAT GW PIP to `local.public_ip_definitions`, and use `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_association` to associate it manually.

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] `AuditRequirement = "No"` tag contradicts HIPAA compliance posture

**File:** `terraform/locals.tf:9`

**Issue:** The `common_tags` include `Compliance = "HIPAA"`, `Confidentiality = "PII/PHI"`, `DataSensitivity = "PII/PHI"`, and `RetentionPolicy = "7-years-PHI"`, but also `AuditRequirement = "No"`. HIPAA Security Rule 164.312(b) mandates audit controls -- mechanisms to record and examine activity in information systems containing ePHI. Tagging infrastructure that handles PII/PHI as not requiring audit creates a compliance documentation gap. If an auditor queries Azure Resource Graph filtering on `AuditRequirement == "No"`, these resources will be excluded from audit scope despite handling PHI.

**Evidence:** HIPAA Security Rule 45 CFR 164.312(b): "Implement hardware, software, and/or procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in information systems that contain or use electronic protected health information." A tag stating "No" audit requirement on PHI infrastructure directly contradicts this requirement.

**Fix:** Change to `AuditRequirement = "Yes"` or at minimum `AuditRequirement = "HIPAA-Required"`.

### [SEVERITY: LOW] Hardcoded `sku_name = "Standard"` duplicates module default

**File:** `terraform/natg.tf:9`

**Issue:** The comment says "This is the default for the module, but explicit for clarity." While explicitness is fine, the AVM module's `var.sku_name` defaults to `null`, and when null, Azure defaults to Standard. However, the module passes `sku_name = var.sku_name` directly to the resource. Passing `null` would work, but the explicit "Standard" is actually the safer choice. No change needed -- this is defensive coding. Withdrawing.

**File:** `terraform/main.tf:23-26`

**Issue:** The `moved` block from `azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer-image-natg-ipprefix` to `azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix` is a one-time state migration artifact. After the rename has been applied to all environments (dev, staging, prod), this block serves no purpose and adds noise. Leaving stale `moved` blocks creates confusion for future developers who may think the migration is still pending.

**Evidence:** Terraform documentation: "After you have updated all references to the old address and have applied the change in all configurations, remove the moved block."

**Fix:** Add a comment with a cleanup date, or create a backlog item to remove after confirming all environments have applied the state move:
```hcl
# TODO: Remove after confirming state migration applied to all environments (tracked in backlog)
moved { ... }
```

## Adversarial Code Review -- Round 3 Findings

After thorough analysis of all terraform files, here are the issues that Rounds 1 and 2 missed:

```
[SEVERITY: HIGH] NAT Gateway Public IP Output Lacks Sensitivity Marking
File: terraform/outputs.tf:53-55
Issue: The nat_gateway_public_ip_address output exposes the static public IP
address in plaintext. In a HIPAA-compliant infrastructure handling PII/PHI,
public IP addresses are operational security data -- they reveal the exact
egress point of the network. An attacker knowing this IP can target it for
DDoS or use it to correlate traffic. Other outputs in this codebase already
use sensitive = true for sensitive data, but ALL NAT gateway outputs are
missing this flag.
Evidence: The output block has no sensitive = true attribute. The IP address
is the single static egress point for ALL private subnet traffic including
PII/PHI workloads. The ip_prefix CIDR output (line 58-61) has the same
problem -- it reveals the full /30 prefix range.
Fix: Add sensitive = true to nat_gateway_public_ip_address and
nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_value outputs:
  output "nat_gateway_public_ip_address" {
    sensitive   = true
    description = "..."
    value       = module.avm_nat_gateway.public_ip_resource["packer_image_natg_pip"].ip_address
  }
  output "nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_value" {
    sensitive   = true
    description = "..."
    value       = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix.ip_prefix
  }
```

```
[SEVERITY: HIGH] VNet Module Uses Pessimistic Constraint ~> 0.2 While NAT GW Module Is Exact-Pinned
File: terraform/networking.tf:8
Issue: The virtualnetwork module uses version = "~> 0.2" which allows any
0.2.x patch/minor to be pulled on terraform init. The NAT Gateway module is
exact-pinned to "0.2.1" and the azurerm provider is exact-pinned to
"= 4.25.0". This inconsistency means the VNet module -- which is the
resource that ASSOCIATES the NAT Gateway with subnets -- can silently
upgrade and potentially change subnet-NAT association behavior, input
schemas, or output attribute names. A breaking change in the VNet AVM
module's subnet map schema would sever the NAT Gateway association with
no warning at plan time.
Evidence: version = "~> 0.2" on line 8 of networking.tf vs version = "0.2.1"
on line 3 of natg.tf. AVM modules are pre-1.0 (0.x) where semver minor
bumps CAN be breaking.
Fix: Exact-pin the VNet module to the version currently in the lock file:
  version = "0.2.0"  # or whatever version is in .terraform.lock.hcl
This matches the pinning strategy used for the NAT GW module and azurerm
provider.
```

```
[SEVERITY: HIGH] Hardcoded SecurityContact Email in locals.tf Exposes PII in State and Plans
File: terraform/locals.tf:28
Issue: SecurityContact = "juliano.barbosa@hypera.com.br" is hardcoded as a
tag value. This personal email address is PII under HIPAA. It gets stored
in Terraform state (which is in Azure Storage), appears in every plan
output, and is applied as a tag to EVERY resource. If state or plan logs
leak, this personal contact is exposed. All other sensitive strings in
this codebase (webhook URLs, storage URIs) are passed via variables --
this one is not.
Evidence: Line 28 of locals.tf shows the email hardcoded. The terraform.tfvars
file contains the msteams_channel webhook as a variable but the security
contact is inline. Tags are stored in state as plaintext attributes on
every resource.
Fix: Extract to a variable with sensitive = true:
  variable "security_contact_email" {
    type        = string
    description = "Security contact email for HIPAA compliance tags"
    sensitive   = true
  }
Then reference var.security_contact_email in locals.tf. Note: marking
tags as sensitive has limited effect (Azure stores them as metadata
regardless), but it prevents the value from appearing in CLI plan output
and logs.

```
[SEVERITY: MEDIUM] NAT Gateway Module PIP Created Without lifecycle prevent_destroy
File: terraform/natg.tf:23-27
Issue: The public IP prefix in main.tf has lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }
(correctly added in Round 2), but the NAT Gateway's static Public IP --
created internally by the AVM module via public_ips map -- has NO such
protection. If someone removes the "packer_image_natg_pip" key from the
public_ips map or changes the module version, the PIP is destroyed and a
new IP is allocated. This static IP is likely allowlisted at firewalls and
third-party services (the same rationale documented in main.tf:16-18 for
the prefix). Losing the PIP is equally catastrophic.
Evidence: The AVM module creates the PIP internally -- there is no way to
add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to a module-created sub-resource.
The IP prefix has this protection but the PIP does not.
Fix: Move PIP creation OUT of the AVM module into a standalone
azurerm_public_ip resource (similar to how the IP prefix is managed
externally in main.tf). Then apply lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }.
Use the module's public_ip_association_enabled input or
azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_association to link it. This gives you
full lifecycle control over the PIP. Example:
  resource "azurerm_public_ip" "natg_pip" {
    name                = local.nat_gateway_pip_name
    location            = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.location
    resource_group_name = data.azurerm_resource_group.packer_rg.name
    allocation_method   = "Static"
    sku                 = "Standard"
    tags                = local.common_tags
    lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }
  }
```

Evidence: The moved block on lines 23-26 is a one-shot operation. There is

catastrophic.
Fix: Add an import block alongside the moved block for disaster recovery

after first successful apply:

  moved {
    from = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer-image-natg-ipprefix
    to   = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix
  }
  # Disaster recovery: if state is lost, import with:

[SEVERITY: MEDIUM] No Explicit Dependency Between IP Prefix Association and VNet NAT Gateway Reference
File: terraform/natg.tf:48-51 and terraform/networking.tf:42-44,64-66
Issue: The azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_association resource
(natg.tf:48) and the subnet nat_gateway associations (networking.tf:42,64)
both depend on module.avm_nat_gateway.resource_id, but there is no
explicit dependency ensuring the prefix association completes BEFORE
subnets start using the NAT gateway. Terraform may apply the subnet
associations in parallel with (or before) the prefix association. This
means subnets could briefly route through the NAT gateway using ONLY
the single PIP without the prefix, creating a window where outbound
traffic uses an IP not in the expected prefix range. For HIPAA
allowlisting at downstream services, this transient state could cause
connectivity failures.
Evidence: No depends_on between the VNet module and the prefix
association resource. Terraform's implicit dependency graph only sees
that both depend on the NAT GW module output, not on each other.
Fix: Add depends_on to the virtualnetwork module:
  module "virtualnetwork" {
    ...
    depends_on = [
      azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_association.natg_prefix
    ]
  }
```

[SEVERITY: LOW] terraform.tfvars Contains Subscription ID in custom_image_id -- No Variable Validation
File: terraform/terraform.tfvars:40
Issue: custom_image_id contains an embedded Azure subscription ID
(e214e4b4-b890-4b78-8e54-6b32b80acc77). The variable declaration
(variables.tf:104-107) has no validation block to ensure the format is
a valid Azure resource ID. If someone pastes a malformed ID, the error
surfaces only at apply time from Azure API, not at plan time from
Terraform validation. While not NAT Gateway specific, it was introduced
in the same scope and follows the pattern of validation blocks added
to all other NAT variables.
Evidence: All NAT-related variables (lines 159-229) have validation blocks.
custom_image_id on line 104-107 has none. The subscription ID embedded in
the value is also not parameterized.
Fix: Add validation to custom_image_id:
  variable "custom_image_id" {
    type        = string
    description = "Resource ID of the custom VM image"
    validation {
      condition     = can(regex("^/subscriptions/[a-f0-9-]+/resourceGroups/.+/providers/Microsoft.Compute/images/.+$", var.custom_image_id))
      error_message = "custom_image_id must be a valid Azure Compute image resource ID."
    }
  }
```

## Adversarial Code Review -- Round 3 Findings

After examining the actual AVM module source code (v0.2.1), all Terraform configuration files, variable definitions, and cross-referencing against Azure NAT Gateway documentation and HIPAA compliance requirements, here are the NEW issues that Rounds 1 and 2 missed.

---

### [SEVERITY: HIGH] NAT Gateway missing resource lock -- IP prefix protected but NAT GW itself is not

**File:** `terraform/natg.tf:1-43`

**Issue:** The `azurerm_public_ip_prefix` resource has `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` (good), but the NAT Gateway itself has no protection. The AVM module exposes a `lock` variable (type `object({ kind = string, name = optional(string) })`) that is not being used. If someone runs `terraform destroy` or removes the module block, the NAT Gateway and its module-managed PIP are destroyed without guardrails. In a HIPAA environment where the NAT GW's static IP is allowlisted at firewalls for PII/PHI data flows, destroying the NAT GW is equally catastrophic as destroying the IP prefix -- all outbound connectivity for both subnets dies instantly.

**Evidence:** AVM module `variables.tf` line 35-52 shows `var.lock` accepting `CanNotDelete` or `ReadOnly`. The IP prefix has `prevent_destroy` but the NAT Gateway that depends on it does not. Azure best practice for production networking resources is to apply `CanNotDelete` locks.

**Fix:** Add the lock parameter to the AVM module call:
```hcl
lock = {
  kind = "CanNotDelete"
  name = "natg-protect-hipaa-outbound"
}
```

---

### [SEVERITY: HIGH] Sensitive PII in common_tags exposed via outputs without sensitive flag

**File:** `terraform/outputs.tf:48-66` and `terraform/locals.tf:1-33`

**Issue:** `local.common_tags` contains `SecurityContact = "juliano.barbosa@hypera.com.br"` (a personal email address -- PII under HIPAA/LGPD). Every output that exposes a full resource object (like the NAT Gateway module outputs) can leak these tags in Terraform state and `terraform output` commands. While the individual outputs defined here only extract scalar values (IDs, IPs), the Terraform state file itself stores the full module resource attributes including all tags. More critically, the `all_public_ip_addresses`, `all_public_ip_ids`, and `all_public_ip_fqdns` outputs at lines 79-99 expose resource details without the `sensitive = true` flag. Anyone with state file access sees the email address in tag metadata.

**Evidence:** The `SecurityContact` tag contains a named individual's corporate email. Under HIPAA, organizational contact information tied to specific individuals constitutes workforce PII. The Terraform remote state in Azure Storage is accessible to anyone with the storage account key (which is passed via `-backend-config`).

**Fix:** Either (a) change `SecurityContact` to a distribution list (`infra-so@hypera.com.br`) instead of a named individual, or (b) add `sensitive = true` to outputs that could leak state, or (c) ensure the state backend storage account has proper RBAC and encryption-at-rest (verify separately).

---

### [SEVERITY: HIGH] VNet module version constraint is too loose -- allows breaking upgrades

**File:** `terraform/networking.tf:8`

**Issue:** The VNet module uses `version = "~> 0.2"` which allows any `0.x` minor version bump (0.3, 0.4, etc. up to 1.0). For AVM modules in the 0.x range, minor versions CAN include breaking changes per SemVer pre-1.0 rules ("Major version zero (0.y.z) is for initial development. Anything MAY change at any time"). Meanwhile, the NAT Gateway module is correctly pinned to exact version `0.2.1`. This inconsistency means `terraform init -upgrade` could pull a breaking VNet module version that changes subnet association behavior, potentially detaching the NAT Gateway from subnets and breaking HIPAA-compliant outbound routing.

**Evidence:** SemVer spec section 4: "Major version zero (0.y.z) is for initial development. Anything MAY change at any time. The public API SHOULD NOT be considered stable." The NAT GW module is pinned (`= "0.2.1"`) but the VNet module is not (`~> 0.2`), creating an asymmetric risk.

**Fix:** Pin the VNet module to an exact version:
```hcl
version = "0.2.0"  # or whatever the current resolved version is
```

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] NSG modules also use loose version pinning

**File:** `terraform/nsg.tf:3,30,55`

**Issue:** All three NSG AVM modules use `version = "0.4.0"` which IS exact-pinned (good). However, this is worth confirming the pinning is intentional vs. coincidental. No issue here on re-inspection -- withdrawing this finding.

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] No explicit deny-all outbound NSG rule on PrivateSubnet -- NAT GW provides unrestricted egress

**File:** `terraform/nsg.tf:1-25` and `terraform/terraform.tfvars:43-66`

**Issue:** The PrivateSubnet's NSG (`private_security_rules` in tfvars) only has two inbound rules (`Allow-Internal` at priority 100, `Allow-HTTPS` at priority 110). There are zero outbound rules defined. Azure NSGs have a default outbound rule (priority 65000) that allows ALL outbound traffic. Combined with the NAT Gateway association, this means ANY workload in the PrivateSubnet can reach ANY internet destination. For a HIPAA environment handling PII/PHI, the principle of least privilege demands explicit outbound restrictions. A compromised Packer build VM could exfiltrate data to any arbitrary endpoint.

**Evidence:** Azure documentation: "Default outbound security rules -- AllowInternetOutBound: Allow all outbound Internet traffic." HIPAA Security Rule 164.312(e)(1) requires technical security measures to guard against unauthorized access to ePHI transmitted over electronic communications networks. Unrestricted outbound through NAT GW violates this.

**Fix:** Add explicit outbound deny-all rule at a lower priority and only allow required destinations:
```hcl
{
  name                       = "Deny-All-Outbound"
  priority                   = 4096
  direction                  = "Outbound"
  access                     = "Deny"
  protocol                   = "*"
  source_port_range          = "*"
  destination_port_range     = "*"
  source_address_prefix      = "*"
  destination_address_prefix = "Internet"
  description                = "HIPAA: deny unrestricted outbound; allow only explicitly permitted destinations"
}
```
Then add specific allow rules for required destinations (Azure services, package repos, etc.) at higher priority.

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] Public IP prefix association creates implicit dependency but no explicit depends_on

**File:** `terraform/natg.tf:48-51`

**Issue:** The `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_association` references both `module.avm_nat_gateway.resource_id` and `azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix.id`. While Terraform can infer these data dependencies, the AVM module internally creates its own `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_association` for the PIP (line 36-41 of module's main.tf). If both the module's internal PIP association AND the external prefix association are created simultaneously during initial apply, Azure sometimes returns a conflict error (409) because two association operations hit the NAT Gateway concurrently. This is a race condition that manifests intermittently.

**Evidence:** Azure ARM has a concurrency limit on NAT Gateway association operations. The module's `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_association.this` and the root's `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_association.natg_prefix` both modify the same NAT Gateway resource. Terraform's parallelism can submit them simultaneously.

**Fix:** Add an explicit dependency to serialize the association operations:
```hcl
resource "azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_association" "natg_prefix" {
  nat_gateway_id      = module.avm_nat_gateway.resource_id
  public_ip_prefix_id = azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix.id

  depends_on = [module.avm_nat_gateway]
}
```
The `depends_on` on the full module ensures all internal module resources (including the PIP association) complete before the prefix association begins.

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] Acceptance Criterion 4 not met -- NAT GW PIP not in `local.public_ip_definitions` for_each

**File:** `terraform/locals.tf:35-41` and `terraform/public_ips.tf:1-15`

**Issue:** Story acceptance criterion 4 states: "Public IPs via `for_each` over `local.public_ip_definitions` with Standard SKU, Static, Regional, IPv4." Currently, `local.public_ip_definitions` only contains the bastion PIP. The NAT Gateway PIP is created inside the AVM module via `public_ips` map, NOT via the `for_each` over `local.public_ip_definitions`. This means the NAT GW PIP does not follow the same pattern as other PIPs in the project, and technically does not satisfy AC4 as written. The NAT GW PIP's configuration (SKU, allocation, tier) is governed by `public_ip_configuration` in the module rather than the standardized `azurerm_public_ip.pips` resource.

**Evidence:** AC4 explicitly says "Public IPs via `for_each` over `local.public_ip_definitions`". The NAT GW PIP is created by the AVM module, not by this for_each. Whether this is acceptable depends on whether AC4 was intended to cover ALL PIPs or only non-module PIPs. This ambiguity should be resolved.

**Fix:** Either (a) clarify in the story that AC4 applies only to standalone PIPs (not module-managed ones), or (b) remove `public_ips` from the AVM module call, add the NAT GW PIP to `local.public_ip_definitions`, and use `azurerm_nat_gateway_public_ip_association` to associate it manually.

---

### [SEVERITY: MEDIUM] `AuditRequirement = "No"` tag contradicts HIPAA compliance posture

**File:** `terraform/locals.tf:9`

**Issue:** The `common_tags` include `Compliance = "HIPAA"`, `Confidentiality = "PII/PHI"`, `DataSensitivity = "PII/PHI"`, and `RetentionPolicy = "7-years-PHI"`, but also `AuditRequirement = "No"`. HIPAA Security Rule 164.312(b) mandates audit controls -- mechanisms to record and examine activity in information systems containing ePHI. Tagging infrastructure that handles PII/PHI as not requiring audit creates a compliance documentation gap. If an auditor queries Azure Resource Graph filtering on `AuditRequirement == "No"`, these resources will be excluded from audit scope despite handling PHI.

**Evidence:** HIPAA Security Rule 45 CFR 164.312(b): "Implement hardware, software, and/or procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in information systems that contain or use electronic protected health information." A tag stating "No" audit requirement on PHI infrastructure directly contradicts this requirement.

**Fix:** Change to `AuditRequirement = "Yes"` or at minimum `AuditRequirement = "HIPAA-Required"`.

---

### [SEVERITY: LOW] Hardcoded `sku_name = "Standard"` duplicates module default

**File:** `terraform/natg.tf:9`

**Issue:** The comment says "This is the default for the module, but explicit for clarity." While explicitness is fine, the AVM module's `var.sku_name` defaults to `null`, and when null, Azure defaults to Standard. However, the module passes `sku_name = var.sku_name` directly to the resource. Passing `null` would work, but the explicit "Standard" is actually the safer choice. No change needed -- this is defensive coding. Withdrawing.

---

### [SEVERITY: LOW] `moved` block is technical debt that should be cleaned up post-apply

**File:** `terraform/main.tf:23-26`

**Issue:** The `moved` block from `azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer-image-natg-ipprefix` to `azurerm_public_ip_prefix.packer_image_natg_ipprefix` is a one-time state migration artifact. After the rename has been applied to all environments (dev, staging, prod), this block serves no purpose and adds noise. Leaving stale `moved` blocks creates confusion for future developers who may think the migration is still pending.

**Evidence:** Terraform documentation: "After you have updated all references to the old address and have applied the change in all configurations, remove the moved block."

**Fix:** Add a comment with a cleanup date, or create a backlog item to remove after confirming all environments have applied the state move:
```hcl
# TODO: Remove after confirming state migration applied to all environments (tracked in backlog)
moved { ... }
```

# Adversarial Code Review -- Round 3
## Story 1-3: Deploy NAT Gateway and Public IP Resources

The popular narrative after two review rounds is "we caught everything." The data says otherwise. Here are 8 issues the prior reviews missed.

---

```
[SEVERITY: HIGH] NAT Gateway outputs expose public IP addresses without sensitive marking
File: terraform/outputs.tf:53-61
Issue: nat_gateway_public_ip_address and nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_value expose the
       exact public IPs used for all outbound HIPAA/PII traffic. These outputs have no
       `sensitive = true` flag. Anyone running `terraform output` or consuming this module
       as a child module sees the NAT egress IPs in plaintext in CI logs, state file
       references, and plan output.
Evidence: The project tags declare Compliance = "HIPAA" and DataSensitivity = "PII/PHI".
          NAT GW public IPs are the fixed egress identity for all PHI-bearing outbound
          connections. Exposing these IPs in CI/CD logs enables targeted attacks against
          the known egress point. Azure security benchmarks recommend treating egress IP
          infrastructure as sensitive. Compare: no other output in this file uses
          `sensitive = true` either, but the NAT GW IPs are the highest-value targets
          because they identify the network perimeter.
Fix: Add `sensitive = true` to nat_gateway_public_ip_address, nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_value,
     and nat_gateway_public_ip_prefix_id. Ensure CI pipelines mask these values. Example:

     output "nat_gateway_public_ip_address" {
       description = "The IP address of the Public IP created by the AVM NAT Gateway module."
       value       = module.avm_nat_gateway.public_ip_resource["packer_image_natg_pip"].ip_address
       sensitive   = true
     }
```

```
[SEVERITY: HIGH] No diagnostic settings on NAT Gateway -- HIPAA audit trail gap
File: terraform/natg.tf (entire file)
Issue: The AVM NAT Gateway module v0.2.1 supports a `diagnostic_settings` input parameter
       for configuring Azure Monitor diagnostic logs and metrics. This is not configured.
       NAT Gateway produces "DDoSProtectionNotifications", "DDoSMitigationFlowLogs",
       "DDoSMitigationReports", and critical metrics like SNATConnectionCount,
       DatapathAvailability, ByteCount, and PacketCount. None of these are being captured.
Evidence: HIPAA Security Rule 164.312(b) requires audit controls to record and examine
          activity in systems containing ePHI. The NAT Gateway is the sole egress path
          for two subnets handling PII/PHI data. Without diagnostic settings, there is no
          audit trail for outbound connection patterns, no alerting on SNAT exhaustion,
          and no evidence for compliance audits. A grep for "diagnostic" across all .tf
          files returns zero results in resource definitions.
Fix: Add diagnostic_settings to the AVM module to ship logs and metrics to a Log Analytics
     workspace or Storage Account:

     diagnostic_settings = {
       natg_diag = {
         name                       = "natg-diag-${var.environment}"
         log_analytics_workspace_id = azurerm_log_analytics_workspace.example.id
         metric_categories          = ["AllMetrics"]
       }
     }

     If no Log Analytics workspace exists yet, this is a prerequisite that should be
     created or referenced as a data source.
```

```
[SEVERITY: HIGH] No NSG flow logs configured -- network forensics blind spot
File: terraform/nsg.tf (entire file)
Issue: Three NSGs (private, public, bastion) are deployed but none have NSG flow logs
       enabled. The docs/nsg-rules.md documentation claims "Diagnostic settings for NSG
       flow logs" are configured, but this is documentation lying about the actual state.
       No azurerm_network_watcher_flow_log resource exists anywhere in the codebase.
Evidence: NSG flow logs are the only way to get Layer-4 visibility into traffic flowing
          through subnets. For a HIPAA environment, you need to demonstrate you can detect
          and investigate unauthorized access attempts. Without flow logs, if a VM in
          PrivateSubnet exfiltrates PHI through the NAT Gateway, there is zero forensic
          evidence of the connection. The NAT GW diagnostic logs alone do not show
          source/destination pairs at the VM level -- only aggregate SNAT counts.
Fix: Deploy NSG flow logs for at least the private and public NSGs:

     resource "azurerm_network_watcher_flow_log" "private_nsg_flow" {
       name                 = "flowlog-${local.nsg_private_name}"
       network_watcher_name = azurerm_network_watcher.example.name
       resource_group_name  = "NetworkWatcherRG"
       network_security_group_id = module.private_nsg_avm.resource_id
       storage_account_id   = azurerm_storage_account.flow_logs.id
       enabled              = true
       retention_policy {
         enabled = true
         days    = 2555  # 7 years per RetentionPolicy tag
       }
       traffic_analytics { ... }
     }
```

```
[SEVERITY: MEDIUM] GatewaySubnet allocated /22 (1024 IPs) -- massive over-provisioning
File: terraform/terraform.tfvars:29
Issue: vnet_subnet_gateway is ["10.248.0.0/22"], reserving 1024 addresses. GatewaySubnet
       is used for Azure VPN Gateway or ExpressRoute Gateway, neither of which are deployed
       in this configuration. Even if deployed, Microsoft recommends /27 as the minimum
       GatewaySubnet size. A /22 wastes 992 addresses from the /20 VNet (4096 total).
Evidence: The VNet is 10.248.0.0/20 = 4096 addresses. Current allocation:
          - GatewaySubnet: /22 = 1024 (25% of VNet, zero resources deployed)
          - PublicSubnet:  /22 = 1024 (25% of VNet)
          - BastionSubnet: /26 = 64
          - PrivateSubnet: /24 = 256
          Total allocated: 2368/4096. The GatewaySubnet alone consumes 25% of the VNet
          for a subnet with zero resources. This blocks future subnet creation for
          additional workloads (e.g., Private Endpoints subnet, AKS subnet).
Fix: Reduce GatewaySubnet to /27 (32 addresses) which is Microsoft's recommended minimum.
     Reclaim the address space for future use:

     vnet_subnet_gateway = ["10.248.0.0/27"]

     If VPN/ExpressRoute is planned, /26 provides comfortable headroom. /22 is never
     justified for GatewaySubnet.
```

```
[SEVERITY: MEDIUM] Telemetry inconsistency across AVM modules -- data leak or policy violation
File: terraform/natg.tf:11, terraform/bastion.tf:6, terraform/networking.tf:10
Issue: enable_telemetry is explicitly set to false on the NAT Gateway module, explicitly
       set to true on the Bastion module, and commented out (defaulting to true) on the
       VNet module. AVM telemetry sends deployment metadata to Microsoft. In a HIPAA
       environment with PII/PHI tags, there should be a consistent policy.
Evidence: Three AVM modules, three different telemetry states:
          - avm_nat_gateway:    enable_telemetry = false (explicit)
          - azure_bastion:      enable_telemetry = true  (explicit)
          - virtualnetwork:     # enable_telemetry = false (commented out, defaults true)
          If the policy is to disable telemetry for HIPAA compliance, bastion and VNet
          are violating it. If telemetry is acceptable, NAT GW is unnecessarily disabled.
          Either way, inconsistency suggests no deliberate decision was made.
Fix: Create a local or variable for telemetry policy and apply consistently:

     variable "enable_avm_telemetry" {
       type    = bool
       default = false  # Disabled for HIPAA compliance
     }

     Then use var.enable_avm_telemetry in all AVM module calls.
```

```
[SEVERITY: MEDIUM] Public IP prefix has prevent_destroy but NAT Gateway PIP does not
File: terraform/natg.tf:22-27 vs terraform/main.tf:14-18
Issue: The IP prefix has lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } because "if these IPs are
       allowlisted at firewalls, destruction is catastrophic." But the individual NAT
       Gateway Public IP created by the AVM module (packer_image_natg_pip) has NO
       lifecycle protection. This PIP is equally critical -- it is the primary outbound
       IP that gets allowlisted. Destroying it allocates a new random IP, breaking all
       firewall rules just as badly as losing the prefix.
Evidence: The AVM module creates the PIP internally. The module's public_ips map creates
          an azurerm_public_ip resource inside the module. You cannot add lifecycle blocks
          to resources inside a module from outside. This means the NAT GW's primary PIP
          is unprotected against accidental destruction, while the prefix (secondary) is
          protected. The most critical IP has the least protection.
Fix: Two options:
     1. Extract PIP creation from the AVM module -- manage it externally in public_ips.tf
        with lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }, then pass it to the AVM module via
        the module's existing mechanism for associating external PIPs (if supported).
     2. If the AVM module does not support external PIP association, document this as an
        accepted risk and add a Terraform Cloud/Enterprise policy (Sentinel/OPA) that
        prevents destruction of resources matching "natg-pip-*".
```

```
[SEVERITY: MEDIUM] PrivateSubnet missing Microsoft.ServiceBus service endpoint
File: terraform/networking.tf:68-72 vs terraform/networking.tf:46-51
Issue: PublicSubnet has service_endpoints for Microsoft.ServiceBus but PrivateSubnet does
       not. If any workload in PrivateSubnet needs to communicate with Azure Service Bus,
       traffic will route through the NAT Gateway over the public internet instead of
       staying on the Azure backbone. For a HIPAA environment handling PII/PHI, this
       means message queue data potentially traverses the public internet.
Evidence: The comment on PrivateSubnet (line 54-56) explicitly states service endpoints
          were added "to prevent PII/PHI traffic from traversing the public internet."
          But the implementation is inconsistent -- Service Bus was included in
          PublicSubnet but omitted from PrivateSubnet with no documented rationale.
          If Service Bus is needed for PublicSubnet workloads, it is likely needed for
          PrivateSubnet workloads too (or it should be removed from PublicSubnet if
          truly unused).
Fix: Either add Microsoft.ServiceBus to PrivateSubnet service_endpoints:

     service_endpoints = [
       "Microsoft.ContainerRegistry",
       "Microsoft.KeyVault",
       "Microsoft.ServiceBus",
       "Microsoft.Storage"
     ]

     Or add an ADR comment explaining why PrivateSubnet intentionally excludes it.
```

```
[SEVERITY: LOW] VNet module version constraint uses pessimistic operator allowing minor upgrades
File: terraform/networking.tf:8
Issue: The VNet module uses version = "~> 0.2" which allows any 0.x.y where x >= 2.
       In semver, 0.x versions have no stability guarantee -- 0.3.0 could contain
       breaking changes. Meanwhile, the NAT Gateway module is correctly pinned to
       exact version "0.2.1". The NSG module is pinned to "0.4.0". The VNet module
       is the only AVM module with a loose constraint.
Evidence: Azure Verified Modules in the 0.x range frequently introduce breaking changes
          between minor versions. A terraform init on a fresh environment could pull
          0.3.0 or 0.4.0 with different subnet configuration schemas, breaking the
          deployment. The NAT Gateway module was deliberately pinned (comment says
          "Pinning to a specific version"), but this discipline was not applied to
          the VNet module which is equally critical.
Fix: Pin to exact version:

     version = "0.2.0"  # or whatever specific version is currently in use

     Check .terraform.lock.hcl for the currently locked version and pin to that.
```

---

## Summary of Findings

| # | Severity | Issue |
|---|----------|-------|
| 1 | HIGH | NAT GW outputs expose public IPs without sensitive flag |
| 2 | HIGH | No diagnostic settings on NAT Gateway -- HIPAA audit gap |
| 3 | HIGH | No NSG flow logs -- network forensics blind spot |
| 4 | MEDIUM | GatewaySubnet /22 wastes 25% of VNet address space |
| 5 | MEDIUM | AVM telemetry inconsistency across modules |
| 6 | MEDIUM | NAT GW PIP lacks prevent_destroy while prefix has it |
| 7 | MEDIUM | PrivateSubnet missing ServiceBus service endpoint |
| 8 | LOW | VNet module version not pinned like other AVM modules |

The conventional wisdom after two review rounds was that this configuration was production-ready. The evidence shows three HIGH-severity gaps in HIPAA compliance posture (no diagnostic logging, no flow logs, exposed IPs) that neither prior review caught. These are not cosmetic -- they represent audit failures in a real HIPAA assessment.

COMPLETED: Eight new issues found challenging post-review readiness narrative with evidence.</result>

        the module's existing mechanism for associating external PIPs (if supported).
     2. If the AVM module does not support external PIP association, document this as an
        accepted risk and add a Terraform Cloud/Enterprise policy (Sentinel/OPA) that
        prevents destruction of resources matching "natg-pip-*".
```

```
[SEVERITY: MEDIUM] PrivateSubnet missing Microsoft.ServiceBus service endpoint
File: terraform/networking.tf:68-72 vs terraform/networking.tf:46-51
Issue: PublicSubnet has service_endpoints for Microsoft.ServiceBus but PrivateSubnet does
       not. If any workload in PrivateSubnet needs to communicate with Azure Service Bus,
       traffic will route through the NAT Gateway over the public internet instead of
       staying on the Azure backbone. For a HIPAA environment handling PII/PHI, this
       means message queue data potentially traverses the public internet.
Evidence: The comment on PrivateSubnet (line 54-56) explicitly states service endpoints
          were added "to prevent PII/PHI traffic from traversing the public internet."
          But the implementation is inconsistent -- Service Bus was included in
          PublicSubnet but omitted from PrivateSubnet with no documented rationale.
          If Service Bus is needed for PublicSubnet workloads, it is likely needed for
          PrivateSubnet workloads too (or it should be removed from PublicSubnet if
          truly unused).
Fix: Either add Microsoft.ServiceBus to PrivateSubnet service_endpoints:

     service_endpoints = [
       "Microsoft.ContainerRegistry",
       "Microsoft.KeyVault",
       "Microsoft.ServiceBus",
       "Microsoft.Storage"
     ]

     Or add an ADR comment explaining why PrivateSubnet intentionally excludes it.
```

```
[SEVERITY: LOW] VNet module version constraint uses pessimistic operator allowing minor upgrades
File: terraform/networking.tf:8
Issue: The VNet module uses version = "~> 0.2" which allows any 0.x.y where x >= 2.
       In semver, 0.x versions have no stability guarantee -- 0.3.0 could contain
       breaking changes. Meanwhile, the NAT Gateway module is correctly pinned to
       exact version "0.2.1". The NSG module is pinned to "0.4.0". The VNet module
       is the only AVM module with a loose constraint.
Evidence: Azure Verified Modules in the 0.x range frequently introduce breaking changes
          between minor versions. A terraform init on a fresh environment could pull
          0.3.0 or 0.4.0 with different subnet configuration schemas, breaking the
          deployment. The NAT Gateway module was deliberately pinned (comment says
          "Pinning to a specific version"), but this discipline was not applied to
          the VNet module which is equally critical.
Fix: Pin to exact version:

     version = "0.2.0"  # or whatever specific version is currently in use

     Check .terraform.lock.hcl for the currently locked version and pin to that.
```

| 1 | HIGH | NAT GW outputs expose public IPs without sensitive flag |
| 2 | HIGH | No diagnostic settings on NAT Gateway -- HIPAA audit gap |
| 3 | HIGH | No NSG flow logs -- network forensics blind spot |
| 4 | MEDIUM | GatewaySubnet /22 wastes 25% of VNet address space |
| 5 | MEDIUM | AVM telemetry inconsistency across modules |
| 6 | MEDIUM | NAT GW PIP lacks prevent_destroy while prefix has it |
| 7 | MEDIUM | PrivateSubnet missing ServiceBus service endpoint |
| 8 | LOW | VNet module version not pinned like other AVM modules |

The conventional wisdom after two review rounds was that this configuration was production-ready. The evidence shows three HIGH-severity gaps in HIPAA compliance posture (no diagnostic logging, no flow logs, exposed IPs) that neither prior review caught. These are not cosmetic -- they represent audit failures in a real HIPAA assessment.
